title: COPU: Conformal Prediction for Uncertainty Quantification in Natural Language Generation
authors: Sean Wang1Yicheng Jiang1Yuxin Tang1Lu Cheng2Hanjie Chen11Rice University2University of Illinois Chicagoseanwang027@gmail.comhanjie@rice.edu
abstract: Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) for Natural Language Generation (NLG) is crucial for assessing the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs), as it reveals confidence in predictions, identifies failure modes, and gauges output reliability. Conformal Prediction (CP), a model-agnostic method that generates prediction sets with a specified error rate, has been adopted for UQ in classification tasks, where the size of the prediction set indicates the model’s uncertainty. However, when adapting CP to NLG, the sampling-based method for generating candidate outputs cannot guarantee the inclusion of the ground truth, limiting its applicability across a wide range of error rates. To address this, we propose COPU, a method that explicitly adds the ground truth to the candidate outputs and uses logit scores to measure nonconformity. Our experiments with six LLMs on four NLG tasks show that COPU outperforms baseline methods in calibrating error rates and empirical cover rates, offering accurate UQ across a wide range of user-specified error rates.

paragraphs: Large Language Models (LLMs) have exhibited remarkable capabilities across a wide range of tasks, including code generation (Chen et al., 2021), scientific research (Wang et al., 2023), and medical applications (Clusmann et al., 2023). However, LLMs are also prone to producing hallucinations—outputs that are inaccurate or fabricated—which poses significant challenges to their reliability and adoption in critical domains (Huang et al., 2023). To bridge the gap between understanding hallucinations and effectively quantifying model uncertainty (Liu et al., 2024), Conformal Prediction (CP) offers a robust framework for aligning model outputs with confidence levels (Vovk et al., 2005; Angelopoulos and Bates, 2021; Fontana et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2024). Given an error rate, it produces a prediction set that includes the ground-truth answer, with the size of the set reflecting the model’s uncertainty. A larger prediction set indicates higher uncertainty, while a smaller set indicates lower uncertainty (higher confidence). This method requires minimal assumptions about the data distribution, and the coverage guarantee property makes it robust for UQ.

Previous studies have applied CP to quantify the uncertainty of LLMs in classification tasks (Jiang and de Marneffe, 2022). Existing methods (Ye et al., 2024) have extended CP to quantify uncertainty in multiple-choice question-answering (MCQA) tasks. However, as shown in Figure 1, implementing CP in open-ended Natural Language Generation (NLG) tasks is challenging due to the infinite output space, which complicates the measurement of nonconformity across all possible candidate outputs.

Recent works have proposed sampling-based techniques for sequence-level uncertainty estimation (Quach et al., 2024; Kuhn et al., 2023). These methods typically generate multiple candidate outputs from a language model and analyze their nonconformity to approximate uncertainty. Building on these foundations, Su et al. (2024) and Wang et al. (2024) have developed a similar sampling-based CP framework for NLG tasks as Figure Figure 1, which estimates uncertainty across limited samples (e.g., 30 sequences) using self-consistency theory. Nonetheless, these approaches assume the presence of at least one correct answer within the sampled candidate set—an assumption that may not hold in realistic scenarios, which undermines the reliability of these approaches.

To address these limitations, we propose a simple method, reprompting-based COnformal Prediction for Uncertainty quantification (COPU) in NLG. COPU explicitly incorporates the ground truth answer into the candidate set, and leverages a re-prompting strategy to calculate the conformal score for each candidate instance. We send the answers, along with the question, back to the model to obtain the logits of the answer sequences, and utilize each token’s logits to calculate the nonconformity of the sequence. This approach ensures that the ground truth is included in the sampled candidate set and provides more accurate calibration across a wider range of user-specified error rates. The overview of our approach is shown in Figure 2.

We test COPU on four NLG tasks using six prevalent LLMs. We adopt two metrics to evaluate uncertainty estimation: 1) empirical coverage rate (ECR) measures the proportion of instances in which the prediction set contains the ground truth; 2) average prediction set size (APSS) reflects how large each prediction set is on average. We found LLMs exhibit varying APSS which represents uncertainty across different benchmarks, regardless of their prediction accuracy. Our method accurately reflects the variance in UQ and supports a wider range of error rates with fewer sampling times (down to 5 times).

Uncertainty Quantification. Uncertainty quantification in NLG is crucial for high-stakes applications, as it enables models to signal when their predictions may be unreliable, thus bolstering trustworthiness (Hu et al., 2023). Various approaches have been developed to quantify uncertainty, each with unique strengths. Calibration-based methods attempt to push probabilities towards the relative frequencies with which predictions are judged to be correct (Eisape et al., 2020; Dhuliawala et al., 2022; Lee et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023; inter alia). As these methods need additional training and supervision, they are expensive and have been shown to be sensitive to distribution shift (Kuhn et al., 2023). Bayesian-based methods (Ambati, 2012; Fomicheva et al., 2020; Zerva et al., 2022) integrate prior distributions into the model to produce probabilistic estimates, providing a theoretically grounded measure of uncertainty. In practice, however, the posterior is intractable, motivating approximate methods (Baan et al., 2023). Verbalized uncertainty (Tanneru et al., 2024) has also been explored, where the model articulates its confidence in specific responses. However, recent work by Xiong et al. (2024) indicates that LLMs are prone to be overconfident when verbalizing uncertainty, suggesting that verbalized estimations alone may lack reliability.

CP for Uncertainty Estimation. Conformal Prediction (Vovk et al., 2005; Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Angelopoulos and Bates, 2021) is an established framework that converts heuristic uncertainty scores from a model into statistically rigorous uncertainty estimates. Recently, CP has been applied to LLMs for uncertainty estimation (Baan et al., 2023). For example, Kuhn et al. (2023) and Ye et al. (2024) applied CP to quantifying uncertainty in MCQA. Nevertheless, this approach is not feasible for open-ended generation tasks due to the vast output space in NLG, which far exceeds the constrained label space typical of classification.

Recent works have adapted CP to NLG tasks. For instance, Quach et al. (2024) introduced a calibrated stopping rule for generating high-quality output sequences, effectively sampling from the expansive sequence space. Su et al. (2024) further proposed a logits-free CP approach, leveraging sampled responses to approximate the sequence space and incorporating response frequency into the conformal score function. Similarly, Wang et al. (2024) aligned response sampling with uncertainty estimates through black-box UQ, aiming to uphold correctness within limited sampling constraints.

However, these methods assume that every candidate set contains at least one correct answer, which works for multiple-choice tasks but does not hold in language generation. Our approach addresses this by explicitly incorporating the ground truth into the response if it’s not present in the sampled set, enhancing coverage and improving utility in uncertainty quantification.

CP is a distribution-independent and model-agnostic approach to UQ (Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Vovk et al., 2005; inter alia). Instead of producing a one-hot labeled answer towards a question, CP returns a prediction set which contains possible answers with a specified confidence level (e.g., 95%). CP utilizes the size of the prediction set to quantify the model uncertainty.

Let ℳ\mathcal{M} denote a language model. CP works in the following process to create the prediction set for a dataset 𝒞D​a​t​a\mathcal{C}_{Data}: where xt​e​s​t\textbf{x}_{test} represents an instance in the test set, y′\textbf{y}^{\prime} represents a candidate answer sequence from sampled sequence set 𝒴\mathcal{Y}. In this process, CP generates the prediction sets for data in test set 𝒞T​e​s​t\mathcal{C}_{Test}. The average size of these prediction sets, can be used as uncertainty estimation of model ℳ\mathcal{M}.
1. Identify a heuristic notion of uncertainty using the pre-trained model. Typically, it uses the model ℳ\mathcal{M} as this notion.

2. Define the nonconformity score function s​(𝐱,𝐲)∈ℝs(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\in\mathbb{R}, which represents the worst agreement between 𝐱\mathbf{x} and 𝐲\mathbf{y}, where in our setting 𝐱\mathbf{x} denotes the question and 𝐲\mathbf{y} denotes the answer label.

3. Apply the nonconformity score function to all data points in the dataset 𝒞D​a​t​a\mathcal{C}_{Data}, and divide 𝒞D​a​t​a\mathcal{C}_{Data} into two parts based on the division factor β\beta. These two parts are named as calibration set 𝒞C​a​l\mathcal{C}_{Cal} and test set 𝒞T​e​s​t\mathcal{C}_{Test}, respectively.

4. Compute threshold q^\hat{q}, which is the ⌈(n+1)​(1−α)⌉n\frac{\lceil(n+1)(1-\alpha)\rceil}{n} quantile of the values for nonconformity score on calibration set 𝒞C​a​l={s​(x1,y1),s​(x2,y2),…,s​(xn,yn)}\mathcal{C}_{Cal}=\{s(\textbf{x}_{1},\textbf{y}_{1}),s(\textbf{x}_{2},\textbf{y}_{2}),...,s(\textbf{x}_{n},\textbf{y}_{n})\}, where nn corresponds to the size of calibration set 𝒞C​a​l\mathcal{C}_{Cal}, α\alpha denotes the error rate defined by users.
5. Use this quantile to form prediction sets for examples in the test set 𝒞T​e​s​t\mathcal{C}_{Test}:

CP, as discussed in Section 3, is well-suited for tasks with a constrained label space, such as MCQA. However, directly applying it to open-ended NLG tasks is challenging. The reason is that CP requires a predefined label space or a set of specific outputs to analyze the output logits. For example, in the case of the question “Where is the capital city of France?” in Figure 2, NLG tasks do not provide explicit answer options, making it impractical to evaluate all possible generated sequences. In contrast, MCQA tasks are accompanied by predefined answer choices, so in practice we only need to analyze the corresponding probability value for each option in Equation 1. To address the limitation of CP in NLG tasks, we propose COPU, a reprompting-based CP method to estimate possible answer sequences generated in generation tasks. Instead of considering sequences constructed from all possible tokens in the model’s vocabulary as options, we sample KK model responses for each query to form a pruned set of potential responses. For instance, in Figure 2, we sample the answer to the question from the model KK times to obtain a set of sequences, and incorporate the ground truth into the set if it is not shown in the sampled sequences (as the incorporation of Paris in Figure 2). Subsequently, we re-prompt the model with original prompts and sampled answers to obtain products of the probability values for sampled answer tokens and use these values to calculate nonconformity scores within the CP framework. Figure 2 shows the framework of COPU.

subsections.title: 4.1 Problem Formulation
paragraphs: Formally, consider xt​e​s​t\textbf{x}_{test} as an input to a model ℳ\mathcal{M}. We define the output space for a generated sequence of length TT as 𝒴=(𝒱∗)T\mathcal{Y}=(\mathcal{V}^{*})^{T}, where 𝒱∗\mathcal{V}^{*} denotes a token from the vocabulary 𝒱\mathcal{V}. We refer to the set 𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}, which represents the set of generated outputs after sampling KK times, as the candidate set. The candidate set 𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K} will only consist of unique sequences, with any repeated sequences disregarded. Moreover, if the ground truth yt\textbf{y}_{t} is not sampled during the KK iterations, we forcefully add it into candidate set 𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}. The candidate set 𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K} shares properties that it contains ground truth label and each candidate is unique, thus can be applied to CP.

To measure the uncertainty of model ℳ\mathcal{M}, for any given test instance xt​e​s​t\textbf{x}_{test} and its corresponding ground truth label yt\textbf{y}_{t}, CP generates a subset of its sampled candidate set 𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K} called prediction set 𝒞\mathcal{C}(xt​e​s​t\textbf{x}_{test}). Given a user-defined error rate α\alpha, this prediction set shares the property that

Consequently, we use the size of the prediction set 𝒞\mathcal{C}(xt​e​s​t\textbf{x}_{test}) as the representation of model’s uncertainty. A larger set implies greater uncertainty, while a smaller set indicates higher confidence, as fewer candidates are required to satisfy the error rate α\alpha.

subsections.title: 4.2 Nonconformity Measurement
paragraphs: To construct the prediction set 𝒞\mathcal{C}(xt​e​s​t\textbf{x}_{test}), we need to develop a nonconformity score function s​(𝐱,𝐲)∈ℝs(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\in\mathbb{R}. Nonconformity measurement shares as a core element in UQ, as it assesses the deviation of a specific output, with larger scores encoding worse agreement between input 𝐱\mathbf{x} and output 𝐲\mathbf{y}. During our experiment, we adopted Least Ambiguous set-valued Classifiers (LAC) (Sadinle et al., 2018). LAC defines the nonconformity function as:

where f​(𝐱)𝐲f(\mathbf{x})_{\mathbf{y}} stands for the softmax score for a given label 𝐲\mathbf{y}. These two methods are built in setting of classification tasks, as both need the softmax score f​(𝐱)𝐲f(\mathbf{x})_{\mathbf{y}} for a given label 𝐲\mathbf{y} for calculation. However, they cannot be applied to our problem directly, since in setting of natural language generation tasks, we are not explicitly gaining the softmax score for a single label, instead different sequences in candidate set 𝒞K={𝐲𝟏,𝐲𝟐,…,𝐲𝐤}\mathcal{C}_{K}=\{\mathbf{y_{1}},\mathbf{y_{2}},...,\mathbf{y_{k}}\}.

To bridge the gap, we propose a reprompting method which prompts the model again with a new prompt that concatenates the input sequence 𝐱={x1,x2,…,xk}\mathbf{x}=\{x_{1},x_{2},...,x_{k}\} with xi∈𝒱x_{i}\in\mathcal{V} and each candidate sequence 𝐲𝐢={y1,y2,…,ym}\mathbf{y_{i}}=\{y_{1},y_{2},...,y_{m}\} with yj∈𝒱y_{j}\in\mathcal{V}, where 𝒱\mathcal{V} denotes the vocabulary. The new prompt is denoted as 𝐱′={x1,x2,…,xk,y1,y2,…,ym}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}=\{x_{1},x_{2},...,x_{k},y_{1},y_{2},...,y_{m}\}. As we prompt the model with 𝐱′\mathbf{x}^{\prime}, we obtain the probability pip_{i} of each token yiy_{i} in the candidate sequence 𝐲i\mathbf{y}_{i} as

Consequently, we obtain a probability set 𝐩i={p1,p2,…,pm}\mathbf{p}_{i}=\{p_{1},p_{2},...,p_{m}\} for the candidate sequence 𝐲i={y1,y2,…,ym}\mathbf{y}_{i}=\{y_{1},y_{2},...,y_{m}\}. And we can form the function

to represent the score for sequence 𝐲\mathbf{y}. This yields a set 𝒫={f​(𝐱)𝐲𝟏,f​(𝐱)𝐲𝟐,…,f​(𝐱)𝐲𝐦}\mathcal{P}=\{f(\mathbf{x})_{\mathbf{y_{1}}},f(\mathbf{x})_{\mathbf{y_{2}}},...,f(\mathbf{x})_{\mathbf{y_{m}}}\}, which consists of products of probabilities for tokens in a given candidate sequence 𝐲i∈CK{\mathbf{y}_{i}}\in C_{K}. We then apply the softmax function to the set of scores to normalize them into a probability distribution:
This transformation allows for a probabilistic comparison between candidate sequences, ensuring the scores are interpretable as relative likelihoods.
Finally, for a given prompt 𝐱\mathbf{x} and any sequence 𝐲𝐢{\mathbf{y_{i}}} in the candidate set 𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}, we define the function ss as
and we provide the pesudocode in Appendix A.

There is another score function named Adaptive Prediction Sets (APS) Romano et al. (2020), which sums the ranked scores of each label, from the higher to the lower, until reaching the true label. We empirically found LAC worked better so we choose it in our setting. A detailed discussion regarding this decision is deferred to the Appendix B.

subsections.title: 4.3 Uncertainty Quantification
paragraphs: After defining the nonconformity score for COPU, we use this function ss to carry out the UQ for model ℳ\mathcal{M} on the dataset 𝒞D​a​t​a\mathcal{C}_{Data}. We first divide the dataset into two subsets of data named calibration set CC​a​lC_{Cal} and test set CT​e​s​tC_{Test} based on the division factor β\beta. The data points in these two datasets have had their nonconformity scores calculated for each candidate sequence in the candidate set. Then based on the user-specified error rate α\alpha, we compute the ⌈(n+1)​(1−α)⌉n\frac{\lceil(n+1)(1-\alpha)\rceil}{n} quantile of the values for points in calibration dataset CC​a​lC_{Cal} as q^\hat{q}. Finally, for data points in test dataset CT​e​s​tC_{Test} and the threshold q^\hat{q} we obtained in calibration dataset CC​a​lC_{Cal}, we compute the prediction set size |C​(xt​e​s​t)||C(x_{test})| based on Equation 1. The average size of prediction sets calculated by Equation 2 is the uncertainty quantification of the model ℳ\mathcal{M} on the dataset 𝒞D​a​t​a\mathcal{C}_{Data}.

subsections.title: 5.1 Experimental Setup
paragraphs: Models. Given that our method incorporates the use of logits in language models, we selected several open-source LLMs to ensure flexibility and transparency. The models we used include Llama2-7B-hf (Touvron et al., 2023), Llama3-8b (Dubey et al., 2024), Mistral-7B (Jiang et al., 2023), Falcon-7B (Almazrouei et al., 2023), MPT-7B (Databricks, 2023) and Yi-6B (Young et al., 2024).

Datasets. To evaluate our method, we consider open-ended NLG tasks including question answering (QA) and reading comprehension (RC). For QA, following Quach et al. (2024) and Kuhn et al. (2023), we selected the standard benchmarking datasets TriviaQA(Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013). TriviaQA is a challenging reading comprehension dataset containing over 650K question-answer-evidence triples. In our experiment, we selected 10K samples from the training dataset of TriviaQA to carry out our experiment. Different from TriviaQA, WebQuestions focuses on the questions users normaly asked in a search engine, with one or multiple answers. For RC, we used two prevalent datasets SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) and WikiQA (Yang et al., 2015). While WikiQA is traditionally used in open-domain question answering tasks, we adapted it for RC by incorporating retrieved context from relevant articles into the prompt. SQuAD provides shorter, span-based answers directly extracted from text, whereas WikiQA often involves longer or more complex answers that can span multiple sentences. Our approach achieved excellent calibration on both datasets.

Prompts. In general, we developed a k-shot prompting strategy for different datasets, inspired by the in-context learning capabilities of LLMs (Brown et al., 2020). For QA benchmarks, we used kk example QA pairs as prompts. For reading comprehension, we provide a context for each QA pair. More details of this can be found in Appendix C.
Baselines. Baselines include current methods in the field of UQ.
∙\bullet Top-Kwhite\text{K}_{\text{white}}. A logit-based non-CP method. It includes the first KK answers for a prompt in the prediction set.

∙\bullet Conformal Language Modeling (CLM) (Quach et al., 2024). It develops a risk-control framework for sampling responses from contemporary large language models, which calibrates a stopping rule for sampling LM outputs that get added to a growing set of candidates until it is confident that the set covers at least one acceptable response.

∙\bullet Logit-free CP for LLMs (LoFree) (Su et al., 2024). A logit-free CP method which formulates nonconformity measures using both fine-grained uncertainty notions semantic and sample frequency, and can be directly used in CP for NLG tasks.
Top-Kwhite\text{K}_{\text{white}}. A logit-based non-CP method. It includes the first KK answers for a prompt in the prediction set.

Conformal Language Modeling (CLM) (Quach et al., 2024). It develops a risk-control framework for sampling responses from contemporary large language models, which calibrates a stopping rule for sampling LM outputs that get added to a growing set of candidates until it is confident that the set covers at least one acceptable response.

Logit-free CP for LLMs (LoFree) (Su et al., 2024). A logit-free CP method which formulates nonconformity measures using both fine-grained uncertainty notions semantic and sample frequency, and can be directly used in CP for NLG tasks.
Metrics. To ensure the thoroughness of our experiments, we selected the following metrics for evaluation Angelopoulos and Bates (2021):

∙\bullet Empirical Coverage Rate (ECR): This metric quantifies the proportion of instances in which the true answer is included within the predicted set of answers. It serves as an indicator of the model’s ability to capture the correct answer within its predictions, thereby ensuring accurate and reliable coverage across the dataset.

∙\bullet Average Prediction Set Size (APSS): This metric (Equation 2) calculates the average number of possible answers included in the model’s prediction set. More specifically, it is calculated by dividing the average number of predicted answers by the average size of the sampled answers, taking into account variations in the number of sampled answers across different prompts.

Empirical Coverage Rate (ECR): This metric quantifies the proportion of instances in which the true answer is included within the predicted set of answers. It serves as an indicator of the model’s ability to capture the correct answer within its predictions, thereby ensuring accurate and reliable coverage across the dataset.

Average Prediction Set Size (APSS): This metric (Equation 2) calculates the average number of possible answers included in the model’s prediction set. More specifically, it is calculated by dividing the average number of predicted answers by the average size of the sampled answers, taking into account variations in the number of sampled answers across different prompts.

subsections.title: 5.2 Comparison with Baselines
paragraphs: We first carried out our experiments on Llama2-7B-hf (Touvron et al., 2023) with comparison with different baseline methods on TriviaQA. Table 1 shows the results. With a user-specified Error Rate, 1 - Error Rate corresponds to the target coverage rate for the prediction set size we construct. We found that COPU achieves significantly closer ECR to 1 - Error Rate than other baselines. This indicates a more accurate conformal prediction result in prediction set construction, thus the result we gain on APSS is more accurate. We also found that LoFreeCP yields poor ECR performance, suggesting its limitation in uncertainty estimation for NLG tasks .

Also we found that COPU supports a wider range of user-specified error rate comparing to other baseline methods. We discovered that CLM is not applicable across a wide range of error rates, indicating a limitation in its uncertainty estimation. We present the result between ECR and target coverage rate defined as 1 - user-specified error rate in Figure 3. We found that COPU remains close to target coverage rate variation, while Top-Kwhite\text{K}_{\text{white}} and CLM can only provide a limited range and LoFreeCP performs poorly as target coverage rate increases.

subsections.title: 5.3 UQ of LLMs Across Benchmarks
paragraphs: We present the calibration results of COPU for various target error rates across different datasets with Llama3-8B (Dubey et al., 2024) in Figure 4, where the split ratio for the calibration set and estimation set is 1:1. Across varying error rates, COPU consistently achieves empirical error rates that closely align with the estimated values, which provides a reliable estimation of the model’s uncertainty across different benchmarks.

Furthermore, we provide the results of ECR (%) with different models across different datasets, under a specific user-specified error rate value 0.1 in Table 2. COPU achieved a close enough ECR towards the target coverage rate 0.9 which is defined as 1 - user-specified error rate value, highlighting a effectiveness of our method on conformal prediction result. Table 2 provides the corresponding average prediction set size, reflecting the number of predictions the models generate to maintain specified error rate (α\alpha = 0.1).

Our findings on CP for uncertainty estimation reveal that different models exhibit varying levels of uncertainty across specific benchmarks. Notably, Llama-2-7b-hf (Touvron et al., 2023) and Llama-3-8B (Dubey et al., 2024) show relatively larger prediction set sizes across most datasets, particularly in WikiQA, where Llama-2-7b-hf has the largest average set size (4.70), followed closely by Llama-3-8B (4.19). This trend implies that these models require more predictions to achieve the specified error rate, indicating higher uncertainty in their outputs compared to the other models tested. In contrast, models like Mistral-7B-v0.1(Jiang et al., 2023), Falcon-7B(Almazrouei et al., 2023), and Yi-6B(Young et al., 2024) maintain smaller prediction set sizes, suggesting greater confidence in their responses. Yi-6B, in particular, consistently produces the smallest set sizes across all datasets, with values close to or equal to 1.0 for most tasks, which reflects its relatively higher certainty about its predictions.

This analysis highlights how different LLMs vary in their calibration and uncertainty across benchmarks. Models with larger prediction set sizes under the same error rate can be interpreted as more conservative, favoring broader prediction intervals to meet the target error threshold, while those with smaller set sizes reflect more concentrated confidence in their predictions. Such differences are essential for applications that require reliable uncertainty estimation, as they suggest varying degrees of prediction robustness and confidence among models.

subsections.title: 5.4 Ablation Studies
paragraphs: In previous experiments, we used a 1:1 ratio to divide the calibration and test sets. To further explore the impact of different calibration-to-test set ratios on coverage rate, we present the results in Figure 5. The figure demonstrates that most coverage rates closely align with the target, indicating that our calibration method maintains robust performance across varying calibration-test ratios, consistently achieving coverage rates near the desired level.

We also investigated the impact of the number of sampling times on our methods. In our previous experiments, we experimented with five sampling times for candidate sequences, while Su et al. (2024) used thirty sampling times. To demonstrate that such extensive sampling is unnecessary—considering the associated time consumption and high computational costs—we performed an experiment to assess the effects of varying sampling times on our methods, with the results presented in Figure 6. Since different sampling times yield distinct sequences of responses, we followed the approach of Quach et al. (2024), who divided the APSS by the number of unique sequences sampled. Our findings indicate that the cover rate remained close to 90% with a user-specified error rate of 0.1, demonstrating that our method is robust across different sampling times. Additionally, we observed that varying the number of sampling times did not significantly affect the average APSS, further supporting the conclusion that excessive sampling is unnecessary.

Our method provides a theoretical guarantee that for any sampled response to a given open-ended question, at least one candidate will correspond to the ground truth answer by explicitly including the ground truth answer. Our approach effectively addresses the issues highlighted by Wang et al. (2024). Our findings reveal that large language models (LLMs) display notable uncertainty on certain natural NLG benchmarks, underscoring the challenges LLMs face in providing confident solutions to these tasks. Additionally, our experiments demonstrate that the robustness of our method holds across various user-specified error rates for each benchmark. These results emphasize the importance of leveraging logits information in CP for quantifying LLM uncertainty.

Our approach has some limitations. First, it uses sampled sequences as a representation of the unbounded output space, and future work could focus on more precise sequence estimation. Second, our method relies on LLM logits, which may not always be available, especially with black-box models. Future research could explore UQ methods that don’t depend on logits. Finally, the size of prediction sets in CP is closely tied to the choice of nonconformity function, suggesting that optimizing this function for each LLM is a valuable area for future work.

All datasets utilized in this work are publicly available and refer to scenarios commonly encountered in daily life, which serve as human annotation. The rationales generated may occasionally include on-factual information or accuracies. Although it is possible that some model-generated rationales or data instances could contain offensive content, we did not observe such cases during our work. In addition, no personal information was collected, such as demographic details or participant identities, during the experiments.

The prediction sets generated by CP could be significantly influenced by the nonconformity function utilized(Ye et al., 2024). In our paper, we tried two popular nonconformity functions, LAC(Sadinle et al., 2018) and APS(Romano et al., 2020). We provide the Empirical Cover Rate (ECR) and Average Prediction Set Size (APSS) for different datasets in Table 3 and Table 4 across different language models with a user specified error rate α=0.1\alpha=0.1.

We found that different nonconformity functions behave differently in the same dataset and the same model. Specifically, LAC has closer ECR to the target cover rate, indicating this nonconformity function holds better CP performance than the APS. This is because APS method holds a higher APS, thus it yields a higher cover rate, which is less accurate in quantification of uncertainty of LLMs in practice.

[Table: Table 3: The comparison between two different nonconformity functions, LAC and APS, on the Empirical Cover Rate (ECR). We observe that LAC can provide a more accurate ECR, which leads to a better CP performance. We select the split ratio to be 1:1 and the user specified error rate to be 0.1.]

[Table: Table 4: The comparison between two different nonconformity functions, LAC and APS, on the Average Prediction Set Size (APSS). We observe that LAC can provide a smaller APSS value, indicating a more efficient nonconformity function in this case. We select the split ratio to be 1:1 and the user specified error rate to be 0.1.]

In this section, we detail the specific prompt structures employed across different tasks in our experiments. Our approach leverages a k-shot in-context learning strategy, designed to guide the model by providing relevant examples that demonstrate the desired format and content style. By including multiple representative examples, we aim to strengthen the model’s ability to generalize effectively to new queries. Each task type has a tailored prompt formulation, carefully structured to maximize the model’s performance, as outlined below.

subsections.title: C.1 Question Answering (QA) Prompts
paragraphs: For the QA tasks, we structured the prompt with kk example question-answer pairs, where kk is set to 32 in this instance. This approach helps the model understand the expected response format and become familiar with the types of questions it might face, improving its ability to generalize effectively when presented with a new target question. By providing multiple question-answer examples, the model can learn to recognize patterns in both the phrasing of questions and the level of specificity required in answers, allowing it to generate a relevant response for new prompts. Each example in the prompt consists of a straightforward question followed by a direct answer. Following the approach of Quach et al. (2024), we used a 32-shot prompt derived from TriviaQA:

• Answer these questions. Q: Who is the current occupant of Dorneywood in Buckinghamshire? A: george osborne Q: Who is Head of State of New Zealand? A: elizabeth ii Q: On 31 May 2010, the President of which country announced his resignation after local politicians criticised comments he made in relation to overseas military deployments? A: germany Q: Mallard, Mandarin, Red-crested Pochard, Teal, Muscovy and Eider are all types of what? A: duck Q: The capital city of Nunavut, Iqaluit lies on which large island? A: baffin island Q: Who was the youngest brother in the Beach Boys? A: carl wilson Q: With what is FIFA usually associated? A: football Q: In which town in Greater Manchester is the TV series set? A: rochdale Q: Who did US Forbes magazine rank in 2010 as the most powerful woman in the world? A: michelle obama Q: What literally means ‘submission to God’ in Arabic? A: islam Q: The Welsh Highland Railway runs between Porthmadog and which town or city? A: caernarfon Q: Which country was formerly known by the Greeks as Tylos? A: bahrain Q: What type of creature is Pepé Le Pew? A: skunk Q: There were three major sieges in the Boer War – Mafeking, Kimberley and which other? A: ladysmith Q: In 1483, who was appointed the first grand inquisitor of the Spanish Inquisition? A: torquemada Q: Leofric of Mercia was the husband of which famous woman? A: godiva Q: Who is the lexicographer, adjudicator, and longest-serving member of the on-screen team on Countdown? A: susie dent Q: A ’Comedo’ is the medical name for what? A: comedones Q: Which state did frontiersman Davy Crockett represent in the US House of Representatives? A: tennessee Q: Which car company manufactures the Corolla model? A: toyota Q: "Which German social economist of Jewish descent, expelled from Germany and France, co-wrote in London ""The Communist Manifesto"" and ""Das Kapital""?" A: marx Q: In a petrol engine, what component mixes fuel and air? A: carburetor Q: A former software engineer, who created the comic strip character Dilbert? A: scott adams Q: In 1949, who became the youngest player to represent England in test cricket? A: brian close Q: Who was the first man to swim 100 metres in under a minute? A: johnny weissmuller Q: In 2010, what North American horse broke the All-time North American female earnings record with over $6 million, broke two world records for Grade/Group I (for consecutive victories and the All-time North American record number of victories by a filly/mare), and tied the All-time North American record for the number of consecutive victories without defeat? A: zenyatta Q: What was the name of the Cunard ocean liner which was launched on 20th September 1906? A: mauretania Q: What is the lowest level of the Earth’s atmosphere? A: troposphere Q: Which country has the greatest number of ancient pyramids? (hint: They are called the Nubian Pyramids) A: sudan Q: In which game could you be faced with a ’Simple or Double Squeeze’? A: bridge Q: Composite, Shield and Cinder Cone are all types of what? A: volcanoes Q: "Which company uses the advertising slogan ""Spend A Little, Live A Lot""?" A: aldi Q: The ancient Greek writer Apollonius of Perga wrote on which subject? A:

By including these examples, the prompt provides the model with consistent guidance on the expected response structure, including how to interpret various question types and provide concise, direct answers. This approach helps the model focus on delivering accurate responses by building familiarity with the question-answer pattern.

subsections.title: C.2 Reading Comprehension (RC) Prompts
paragraphs: For RC tasks, we modified the prompt format to include a relevant passage followed by kk QA examples derived from that passage. This setup is particularly useful for reading comprehension tasks, as it ensures the model receives the necessary context before answering each question. By embedding kk context-specific question-answer pairs within the prompt (with kk set to 4 here), we provide the model with examples that demonstrate how to interpret the passage and answer questions accordingly. Each prompt begins with a brief context paragraph, followed by kk question-answer pairs specific to that passage, and here is the example from WikiQA with kk set to 4:

• Answer these questions. Context: African immigration to the United States refers to immigrants to the United States who are or were nationals of modern African countries. The term African in the scope of this article refers to geographical or national origins rather than racial affiliation. From the Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965 to 2017, Sub-Saharan African-born population in the United States grew to 2.1 million people. Sub-Saharan Africans in the United States come from almost all regions in Africa and do not constitute a homogeneous group. Question: HOW AFRICAN AMERICANS WERE IMMIGRATED TO THE US Answer: As such, African immigrants are to be distinguished from African American people, the latter of whom are descendants of mostly West and Central Africans who were involuntarily brought to the United States by means of the historic Atlantic slave trade. Context: A pump is a device that moves fluids (liquids or gases), or sometimes slurries, by mechanical action, typically converted from electrical energy into hydraulic energy. Mechanical pumps serve in a wide range of applications such as pumping water from wells, aquarium filtering, pond filtering and aeration, in the car industry for water-cooling and fuel injection, in the energy industry for pumping oil and natural gas or for operating cooling towers and other components of heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems. Question: how a water pump works Answer: Pumps operate by some mechanism (typically reciprocating or rotary), and consume energy to perform mechanical work by moving the fluid. Context: Lolita is a 1962 black comedy-psychological drama film directed by Stanley Kubrick, based on the 1955 novel of the same name by Vladimir Nabokov. The black-and-white film follows a middle-aged literature lecturer who writes as "Humbert Humbert" and has hebephilia. He is sexually infatuated with young, adolescent Dolores Haze (whom he calls "Lolita"). It stars James Mason as Humbert, Shelley Winters as Mrs. Haze, Peter Sellers as Quilty, and Sue Lyon (in her film debut) as Dolores "Lolita" Haze. Question: how old was sue lyon when she made lolita Answer: The actress who played Lolita, Sue Lyon, was fourteen at the time of filming. Context: An antibody (Ab) or immunoglobulin (Ig) is a large, Y-shaped protein belonging to the immunoglobulin superfamily which is used by the immune system to identify and neutralize antigens such as bacteria and viruses, including those that cause disease. Antibodies can recognize virtually any size antigen with diverse chemical compositions from molecules. Each antibody recognizes one or more specific antigens. Question: how are antibodies used in Answer: An antibody (Ab), also known as an immunoglobulin (Ig), is a large Y-shaped protein produced by B-cells that is used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as bacteria and viruses. Context: The Mexican peso (symbol: $; currency code: MXN; also abbreviated Mex$ to distinguish it from other peso-denominated currencies; referred to as the peso, Mexican peso, or colloquially varo) is the official currency of Mexico. The peso was first introduced in 1863, replacing the old Spanish colonial real. The Mexican peso is subdivided into 100 centavos, represented by "¢". Question: HOW MUCH IS CENTAVOS IN MEXICO Answer: The peso is subdivided into 100 centavos, represented by "¢". Context: A glacier cave is a cave formed within the ice of a glacier. Glacier caves are often called ice caves, but the latter term is properly used to describe bedrock caves that contain year-round ice. Question: how are glacier caves formed? Answer:

Context: The Mexican peso (symbol: $; currency code: MXN; also abbreviated Mex$ to distinguish it from other peso-denominated currencies; referred to as the peso, Mexican peso, or colloquially varo) is the official currency of Mexico. The peso was first introduced in 1863, replacing the old Spanish colonial real. The Mexican peso is subdivided into 100 centavos, represented by "¢". Question: HOW MUCH IS CENTAVOS IN MEXICO Answer: The peso is subdivided into 100 centavos, represented by "¢".

Context: A glacier cave is a cave formed within the ice of a glacier. Glacier caves are often called ice caves, but the latter term is properly used to describe bedrock caves that contain year-round ice. Question: how are glacier caves formed? Answer:

This contextual prompting allows the model to understand the structure and purpose of the reading comprehension task. By observing these structured pairs, the model learns to connect specific contextual information with relevant questions, aligning its responses to the passage content. This format is designed to optimize comprehension by emphasizing the relationship between the context and the subsequent answers, enabling more accurate and contextually aware responses.
